source: EU AI Act, Article 25 - Obligations of deployers that are also providers
note: Three scenarios where a deployer becomes a provider, triggering full provider obligations under Articles 8-15. This is one of the most consequential provisions of the Act for organizations deploying third-party or GPAI systems.

scenarios.trigger: The deployer puts their name or trademark on a high-risk AI system already placed on the market or put into service, without prejudice to contractual arrangements stipulating that the obligations are otherwise allocated.
scenarios.plain_english: You take a vendor's AI product and slap your brand on it. You're now responsible as if you built it.
detection_questions: Is the AI system marketed under your organization's name or brand?
Do end users see your brand rather than the original provider's?
Have you white-labeled or relabeled the AI system?
Does the end user believe your organization built or developed the AI system?
Is the original provider's name absent from customer-facing materials?
scenarios.example: A staffing company licenses a vendor's AI screening tool and offers it to clients under the staffing company's brand name.
scenarios.risk_level: HIGH - Full provider obligations apply immediately
scenarios.contractual_note: Article 25(1)(a) allows obligations to be contractually reallocated. However, the branded entity remains the party regulators will hold accountable to end users.

scenarios.trigger: The deployer makes a substantial modification to a high-risk AI system that is already on the market or in service.
scenarios.plain_english: You significantly change how the AI works — retrain it, change its architecture, modify its decision logic. You've now made a new system and you're the provider.
detection_questions: Have you retrained or fine-tuned the AI model on your own data?
Have you modified the model's architecture or decision logic?
Have you made changes that alter the AI system's performance or behavior beyond configuration?
Would the original provider consider your changes a 'substantial modification'?
Have you changed the model's output format, decision boundaries, or scoring thresholds in ways not anticipated by the provider?
Did you perform transfer learning or domain adaptation on the model?
scenarios.example: A bank takes an open-source credit scoring model and fine-tunes it on their transaction data, changing its decision boundaries.
scenarios.risk_level: HIGH - Full provider obligations apply. The modification creates a new system.
common_in: Data science teams retraining models on proprietary data
scenarios.note: The distinction between configuration (changing parameters within provider-defined ranges) and substantial modification (changing the system beyond provider intent) is critical. EU Commission delegated acts on 'substantial modification' criteria are expected. Build flexibility for updated criteria.

substantial_modification_examples: Retraining the model on new data sets
Modifying the decision logic or algorithm
Changing the intended purpose of the system
likely_not_substantial_examples: Adjusting parameters within ranges defined by the provider
Updating the system as specified by the provider
Routine maintenance and bug fixes by the provider
Configuration changes explicitly supported in instructions for use

scenarios.trigger: The deployer modifies the intended purpose of an AI system, including a general purpose AI system, which has not been classified as high-risk, so that it becomes a high-risk AI system in accordance with Article 6.
scenarios.plain_english: You take a general-purpose AI tool (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Copilot) and use it for something that falls under Annex III high-risk categories. The tool wasn't high-risk. Your USE of it made it high-risk. You're now the provider.
detection_questions: Are you using a general-purpose AI system (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Copilot, Llama, Mistral, etc.) for a task that falls under Annex III?
Is the AI system being used for a purpose different from what the original provider intended?
Does your use case involve decisions about people's access to employment, credit, education, essential services, or law enforcement?
Did the original GPAI provider's terms of use anticipate your specific use case?
Are AI outputs being used as inputs to decisions that produce legal effects or similarly significant effects on natural persons?
Is a general-purpose AI system being embedded in a workflow that evaluates, scores, ranks, or filters natural persons?
scenarios.example: An HR team uses GPT-4 via API to screen resumes and rank candidates for engineering roles. GPT-4 is a general-purpose AI system. Resume screening falls under Annex III Category 4 (Employment). The HR team's use has made it high-risk.
scenarios.risk_level: CRITICAL - Most dangerous scenario. Organizations do this unknowingly. No detection mechanism exists in most companies.
common_in: Any organization using GPAI tools for HR, credit, insurance, education, or public services decisions
Teams building internal tools on top of LLM APIs
Business units experimenting with AI without governance review
scenarios.detection_gap: Most organizations have no mechanism to detect this. Acceptable use policies say 'don't do bad things with AI.' They don't say 'if you use this chatbot to score candidates, you just made us a provider of a high-risk AI system under Article 25.'

additional_examples.use_case: Using Claude API to evaluate employee performance reviews
additional_examples.why_triggers: Employee performance evaluation falls under Annex III Point 4. Claude is a GPAI not classified as high-risk. Using it for performance evaluation modifies its intended purpose to a high-risk use.

additional_examples.use_case: Using Gemini to pre-screen insurance applications
additional_examples.why_triggers: Insurance risk assessment for life/health falls under Annex III Point 5(b). Gemini is a GPAI. Deployer becomes provider.

additional_examples.use_case: Using GPT-4 to assess creditworthiness in loan applications
additional_examples.why_triggers: Credit scoring of natural persons falls under Annex III Point 5(a). GPT-4 is a GPAI. Deployer becomes provider.

consequences_of_becoming_provider.summary: When a deployer becomes a provider under Article 25, the former provider's obligations under Articles 8-15 and Article 47 transfer to the new provider. This is a FULL transfer of the compliance burden.
obligations_triggered: Transparency and provision of information to deployers (Article 13)
consequences_of_becoming_provider.original_provider_role: The original provider must cooperate with the new provider, providing necessary information and reasonably expected technical access to facilitate compliance (Article 25(2)-(3)). The original provider is NOT released from their own obligations for the original system unless explicitly agreed.

penalties.non_compliance_with_high_risk_obligation: Up to EUR 15,000,000 or 3% of total worldwide annual turnover, whichever is higher
penalties.note: Penalties apply to the entity that became the provider under Article 25, not the original provider

consequences_of_becoming_provider.timeline_urgency: There is no grace period. Once a trigger condition is met, the deployer IS a provider immediately and must comply with all provider obligations. Retroactive compliance is expected.
